title: Chapter 7 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of Unions & Intersections in TypeScript!

questions.question: Which symbol is used to create a Union Type?
questions.explanation: The pipe `|` symbol signifies a union (OR).

questions.question: If you define `let response: string | number;`, which assignment is completely valid?
questions.explanation: The value `100` matches the `number` part of the union.

questions.question: To type an array that can randomly hold a mix of strings or booleans, you wrap the union in parentheses before the brackets: `(string | _______)[]`
questions.explanation: The syntax `(string | boolean)[]` correctly captures the mixed array concept.

questions.question: If you have `let item: string | number`, why will TypeScript block you from calling `item.substring(0)` instantly?
options: Because at runtime, `item` might actually be a number, which doesn't have a `.substring` method, crashing the app.
Because it assumes it is an object.
Because the string is currently empty.
questions.explanation: TypeScript protects you against potential runtime crashes by only allowing methods that are completely shared across all union types.

questions.question: What is the process of using standard JavaScript checks (like `typeof`) to guarantee to TypeScript exactly what type a union variable currently is?
questions.explanation: Type Narrowing (via Type Guards like an `if` statement) focuses the broad union down into a highly specific, safe type inside that block.

questions.question: Which `if` statement successfully perfectly narrows a `string | number` union to just a string so you can safely use string methods?
questions.explanation: The `typeof item === 'string'` check evaluates mathematically to `true` only if `item` is currently a string.

questions.question: Once inside a successful `if (typeof param === 'string')` block, your editor will accurately autocompleting string methods for that parameter.
questions.explanation: Because TS knows the type is safely narrowed to a string inside that block, Intellisense unlocks.

questions.question: What is the name for types that lock a variable down to the exact, specific value (e.g. `"ON" | "OFF"`) rather than a broad generic type like `string`?
questions.explanation: Literal Types utilize specific strings or numbers as the actual type definition itself.

questions.question: What happens if you assign `let state: "pending" | "done" = "error";`?
options: It successfully stores the error string.
TypeScript throws an immediate compilation error.
questions.explanation: The literal type expects strictly one of those exact string sequences. "error" is not in the union.

questions.question: Literal types are exclusively limited to strings; you strictly cannot use numbers for them.
questions.explanation: Literal types support exact numbers, and even exact booleans! (e.g., `let rating: 1 | 2 | 3;`)

questions.question: What keyword allows you to custom-name any complex type arrangement (like a long Union) for easier reusability instantly?
questions.explanation: The `type` keyword creates Type Aliases. (e.g., `type Status = "ON" | "OFF";`)

questions.question: According to community guidelines, when should you universally favor an `interface` over a `type` alias?
options: When defining simple Unions or Primitives.
questions.explanation: Interfaces are purpose-built for object shapes and provide slightly better extensibility and error messages.

questions.question: Which operator mathematically combines multiple types together securely (a Type Intersection)?
questions.explanation: The single ampersand `&` dictates an Intersection (AND).

questions.question: Given `type Animal = { legs: number }` and `type Pet = { name: string }`, what exactly is the type `type Dog = Animal & Pet;` expecting?
options: An object with EITHER legs OR name.
An object with BOTH legs AND name.
questions.explanation: Intersections demand that the object fully satisfies the property requirements of EVERY intersected type.

questions.question: If you intersect completely contradictory primitive types (e.g., `string & number`), TypeScript generates an impossible `never` type.
questions.explanation: A value cannot physically be an raw string and a raw number simultaneously, so TS correctly resolves it to `never`.

questions.question: A Type _____ (using `typeof`) is the standard conditional expression used to perform Type Narrowing on a Union.
questions.explanation: It guards the inner code block, ensuring it only executes if the type perfectly matches the check.

questions.question: Why is the TS editor beautifully robust when you type `let dir: "up" | "down";` and hit space to start assigning it?
options: It provides a dropdown menu offering strictly just "up" and "down".
It creates an interface named dir.
questions.explanation: Intellisense reads literal unions perfectly and guides you gracefully with dropdowns.

questions.question: Which of these correctly creates a Type Alias for an array of strings?
questions.explanation: The `= string[];` officially assigns the type definition to the new Alias name.

questions.question: A `type` alias can successfully define an object shape exactly like an `interface` does.
options: True, the syntax is almost identical: `type User = { name: string }`.
False, `type` aliases cannot use curly braces.
False, `type` aliases can only define primitive aliases.
False, doing so breaks TypeScript completely.
questions.explanation: Type aliases are heavily used to define complex object shapes, though Interfaces are preferred.

questions.question: Variables of type `never` are extremely useful because you can assign literally whatever data you want to them safely.
questions.explanation: It is completely false because the `never` type indicates an impossible type, so you can safely assign absolutely NO values to it.
